reasoning: kpi = die; dɛbite = neighbor; o ǹ faa o suka = he inherited his car → Tchando’s neighbor died, and he inherited his car.
Tando = rain; dori = fall → rain fell = it rained.
N … = my; pe saaki = my wife; ti yete = our house → My wife swept our house.
Bika = child; kɔɔsi = sold; kɔɔka = hen → The child sold the hen.
Soosada = soldiers; kaate = assemble/gather → The soldiers assembled.
Suka = car; kpi = die/break down → The car broke down.
Ba = they; kaate = gathered; tiibu = tree; band = under → They gathered under the tree.
N = I; daaso = friend; yeentire = hurt → I hurt my friend.
Bisu = children; yɔkɔɔti = played/had fun → The children had fun.
Tiibu = tree; dori = fell; puŋa mii = in the forest → The tree fell in the forest.
N = I; taka = went; n daaso = my friend; yete = house → I went to my friend’s house.
Maari = Marie; pei = lost; fa = found; piisi = money → Marie lost the money, but she found it.
Suka = car; miiki = earlier; pɔmpɔmma = passed by → A car passed by earlier.
Bika = child; dori = fell → The child fell.
N kɔɔka = my hen; taka = went; Yooto yete = Yooto’s house → My hen went to Yooto’s house.
kpi is used broadly for states of ceasing/being ruined: neighbor dies; car dies/breaks down; by extension it expresses torn (shirt), settled (matter), lost (fame), frozen (water).
Bisu = children; kaate = gathered; yete = house; band = under.
N = I; kɔɔsi = sold; n suka = my car.
o daaso = her friend; yɔkɔɔti = played; puŋa = in; Tando = rain.
Ba = they; kɔɔsi = sold; ti kɔɔsu = our hens (kɔɔku/ka ~ hen, -su plural).
N = my; susu = shirt; kpi = ruined/‘die’, used for torn/broken states.
Maari = Marie; daaso = friend; fa = find; faa marks attainment/completion (‘find/get back’).
